event: Transfiguration of Christ on Mount Tabor
symbols: mandorla of light — the almond-shaped aureole surrounding Christ signifies His divine glory breaking through His human nature
white garments of Christ — His clothes shining exceedingly white reveal the uncreated light of the Godhead (divine energies in Eastern theology)
Moses and Elijah flanking Christ — the Law and the Prophets bearing witness to Christ as their fulfillment
mountain — sacred elevated space of divine encounter, recalling Sinai and Horeb
prostrate disciples — human inability to stand before the unveiled divine glory, echoing the fear of God throughout Scripture
decorative border — liturgical framing indicating this is an object of veneration and worship
description: A mosaic icon depicting the Transfiguration. At center, Christ stands within a large almond-shaped mandorla of radiating light, wearing brilliant white robes and holding a cross staff. He is flanked by two standing figures on rocky peaks: at left, an older bearded man in green-brown garments (Moses), and at right, a younger dark-haired figure holding a book in blue-draped clothing (Elijah). Below, three disciples are shown in attitudes of awe and prostration on the rocky mountainside — one shields his face at left, one lies face-down at center, and one falls backward at right.
theological_description: The Transfiguration is among the most theologically dense events in the Gospels. On Mount Tabor, Christ's divine glory — normally veiled by His human nature — shines forth, and His garments become whiter than any earthly bleach could make them (Mark 9:3). The presence of Moses and Elijah signifies that the entire Old Testament — Law and Prophets — converges upon and witnesses to Christ. Moses, who could not see God's face and live (Exodus 33:20), now beholds the incarnate God face to face. Elijah, who encountered God in the still small voice on Horeb (1 Kings 19:12), now stands beside the Word made flesh. The three disciples represent the Church receiving this revelation yet unable to fully comprehend it. In Eastern Orthodox theology, the Tabor Light is understood as the uncreated divine energies of God — not a created phenomenon but God's own self-communication. The event also prefigures Christ's Resurrection glory and the eschatological transformation of all creation. Peter's offer to build three tabernacles (Mark 9:5) reflects the human desire to contain the divine, while the Father's voice from the cloud — 'This is my beloved Son; listen to Him' — establishes Christ's supreme authority over Law and Prophets alike.

scripture_refs.reason: Matthew's account of the Transfiguration on the mountain with Peter, James, and John
Mark's account emphasizing the dazzling whiteness of Christ's garments
Luke's account noting Moses and Elijah spoke of Christ's exodus to be accomplished in Jerusalem
Peter's own testimony recalling the Transfiguration as eyewitness of Christ's majesty
Moses who could not see God's face now beholds the incarnate God in glory
Elijah's theophany on Horeb prefigures his presence at this greater theophany on Tabor
The glorified Christ whose face shines like the sun, echoing the Transfiguration radiance
